nativeRange.habitat: The Butterfly Goodeid inhabits clear, well-oxygenated springs and spring-fed streams in the Jalisco highlands of Mexico, with abundant aquatic vegetation and fast-flowing, moderately hard, alkaline water over gravel and rock substrates.

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, hornwort, anubias, vallisneria)
moderate water flow to mimic spring-fed habitat
refugia areas for females to escape male harassment

diet.notes: Accepts most aquarium foods readily. Offer a varied diet of high-quality flake, spirulina flake, algae wafers, blanched spinach, nori, and lettuce to satisfy the significant plant-matter component of the natural diet. Also relishes live and frozen foods such as daphnia, brine shrimp, and bloodworm. Grazes algae off surfaces when available; ensure greens are provided regularly if algae growth is limited.

goodWith: other cool-tolerant livebearers of similar size
robust tetras and barbs of comparable size
other Goodeidae species in species-biotope setups
avoidWith: small or delicate fish easily harassed
dwarf shrimp (fry and juveniles at risk)
tropical fish requiring consistently high temperatures (26°C+)
Poeciliidae livebearers with elaborate fins (guppies, fancy mollies)

imageCandidates.notes: Marie France Janelle; 944x682. Adult male in-situ clearly showing the diagnostic yellow-fringed caudal fin with black subterminal band and iridescent speckled flanks; sharp, whole fish.
domdomegg; 4592x2576. High-res shoal shot representing how this shoaling species is kept; central fish sharp with typical mottled pattern.
domdomegg; 4592x2576. Sharp lateral view of a single male showing dark-speckled body and fringed caudal fin; part of a small group.

summary: A viviparous Mexican goodeid now Critically Endangered in the wild, the Butterfly Goodeid is a cool-tolerant beginner fish whose embryos are nourished via trophotaeniae — placenta-like structures unique to Goodeidae. Males display yellow-fringed tails and a notched anal fin. Known fin-nippers, they are best kept in species groups or with robust tankmates.
careNotes:
The Butterfly Goodeid is a subtly stunning Mexican livebearer from the Río Ameca drainage of Jalisco, now holding on in tiny, fragmented wild populations and classified as Critically Endangered (IUCN 2018). Captive populations maintained by dedicated hobbyists represent the primary insurance against total extinction, making responsible captive breeding genuinely conservation-important for this species.

Keeping is straightforward for a reasonably experienced beginner. A group of six or more in a 90-litre, 90 cm tank is a comfortable starting point; larger groups in 120+ litre tanks better distribute male aggression. Maintain a 2:1 or 3:1 female-to-male ratio — males can harass females relentlessly. Water quality should be clean, well-oxygenated, and moderately hard to hard (gH 9–20, kH 7–11, pH 7.0–8.0). Unlike most tropical livebearers, the Butterfly Goodeid is native to the cooler Mexican highlands and thrives at 20–25°C; temperatures above 28°C stress these fish. This cool tolerance makes them suitable for lightly heated or unheated aquaria in temperate climates during summer.

The species has a well-earned reputation for fin-nipping — particularly towards long-finned or slow-moving tankmates. Keep with robust, similarly-sized fish or in a species-only setup. Males form a loose dominance hierarchy; while sparring is common, serious fin damage is uncommon in adequately sized, well-planted tanks. Provide dense planting and algae-covered surfaces for cover, supplementary grazing, and visual breaks that diffuse male competition.

Diet is flexible: a base of quality flake and spirulina supplemented with blanched greens (spinach, nori, lettuce), algae wafers, and occasional live or frozen invertebrates covers all nutritional needs. The Butterfly Goodeid is not taxonomically related to Poeciliidae livebearers (guppies, mollies, platies) despite superficial similarities — it belongs to Goodeidae, a family of Mexican highland fish with fundamentally different reproductive biology (see Breeding Notes). The male anal fin bears a distinctive notch, separating the anterior andropodium from the posterior lobe — a reliable field mark distinguishing goodeid males from poeciliid males.
breedingNotes:
The Butterfly Goodeid is a true viviparous livebearer with a reproductive system fundamentally different from Poeciliidae. Rather than the embryo relying primarily on yolk reserves, developing fry are nourished via trophotaeniae — ribbon-like, branching vascular extensions protruding from the embryo’s anal region that interface with the maternal ovarian lining to exchange nutrients and gases, functioning analogously to a placental umbilical cord. This matrotrophy enables the production of large, well-developed fry (up to 20 mm at birth) despite relatively small brood sizes.

Gestation lasts approximately 55–60 days at 22–24°C, producing 5–30 fry per brood (typically 10–20). Newly born fry are large enough to accept crushed adult flake, baby brine shrimp, and micro-pellets immediately. No parental care is provided and adults will consume small fry; dense planting or a breeding box substantially improves survival. Females can produce successive broods every 6–10 weeks under good conditions. Sexual maturity is reached in approximately 6 months.

Unlike Poeciliidae, females cannot store sperm across multiple broods — each brood requires a fresh mating. Males display to females with spread fins and a characteristic lateral quiver; the notched anal fin (andropodium) is used for internal fertilisation. For conservation breeding, maintain studbook-style records if possible and periodically exchange fish with other hobbyist groups to maintain genetic diversity across captive lineages.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Ameca splendens
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provided pH (7.0–8.0), hardness (179–357 ppm ≈ gH 10–20), minimum tank base (90 cm × 30 cm), native range (Río Ameca drainage, Jalisco, including Río Teuchitlán and Sayula valley), diet (omnivore with plant emphasis), temperament (aggressive males, not a typical community fish), and breeding details (55–60 day gestation, 5–30 large fry, trophotaeniae). Temperature listed as 24–24°C — apparent data entry error; cross-referenced against FishBase and Wikipedia for corrected range of 20–26°C.

additional.name: FishBase — Ameca splendens
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Goodeidae, Cyprinodontiformes), max size (males 8.0 cm TL, females 12.0 cm TL), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (9–19 dH), depth range (0–2 m), native range (Jalisco, Mexico), and IUCN status (Critically Endangered, assessed April 2018). FishBase natural temperature 26–32°C appears inconsistent with the highland plateau habitat and Wikipedia captive optimum of 20–25°C; the lower end of 20–26°C used as aquarium range.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Ameca splendens
additional.notes: Confirmed IUCN CR status, historical wild range (Rio Ameca drainage, type locality Río Teuchitlán near Teuchitlán), cool-water tolerance (summer pond keeping down to 15°C overnight air temperatures), captive temperature optimum (20–25°C), adult size (males 7–8 cm, females to 10 cm), fin-nipping reputation, dominance hierarchy among males, trophotaeniae viviparity, gestation 6–10 weeks, fry size up to 20 mm, and sexual maturity at ~6 months.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Ameca splendens assessment
additional.notes: Confirmed Critically Endangered (CR) IUCN listing (B1ab+2ab criteria). Species was previously considered Extinct in the Wild circa 2008 before remnant populations were confirmed; current status reflects the few remaining wild localities in the Río Ameca system.

fish.breedingNotes: The Butterfly Goodeid gives birth to 5–30 large live fry after a gestation of 55–60 days. Rather than relying on yolk alone, developing embryos are nourished through trophotaeniae — ribbon-like vascular structures that connect to the maternal ovarian lining and function similarly to a placenta. Fry are large enough at birth to accept crushed adult foods immediately. Females require fresh mating for each brood, as sperm is not stored between pregnancies. The species breeds readily without special conditioning; providing dense plant cover or separating fry into a breeder box substantially improves survival, as adults will consume small young.
